title: Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity After Peripheral Nerve Block
summary: A 72-year-old woman receiving an interscalene block with ropivacaine 0.5% before total shoulder arthroplasty develops prodromal neurologic symptoms, a generalized seizure, and then ventricular dysrhythmia with hypotension. Learners must recognize LAST, control the seizure, and deliver 20% lipid emulsion with ASRA-modified resuscitation.
learningObjectives: Recognize the prodromal neurologic signs of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (perioral numbness, tinnitus, metallic taste, agitation) during and after peripheral nerve block placement.
Institute immediate first-response measures: stop the injection, call for help and the LAST rescue kit, and support the airway with 100% oxygen to prevent hypoxia and acidosis.
Treat local anesthetic-induced seizures with benzodiazepines while avoiding large propofol doses in the hemodynamically compromised patient.
Administer 20% lipid emulsion correctly: 1.5 mL/kg bolus over 2-3 minutes followed by an infusion, with appropriate redosing and maximum-dose awareness.

Apply ASRA-modified ACLS for LAST: reduced epinephrine bolus doses (1 mcg/kg or less), avoidance of vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and lidocaine, and early notification for cardiopulmonary bypass capability.
Plan appropriate post-event monitoring for at least 4-6 hours given the risk of recurrent cardiovascular instability.
setup: Configure the room as a preoperative block bay: stretcher, standard monitors, nasal cannula with capnography, ultrasound machine with a linear probe at the bedside, and a running 20 g peripheral IV.
Stage a LAST rescue kit (mock 20% lipid emulsion 500 mL bags, syringes, ASRA checklist card, airway equipment) in an adjacent room; it arrives only when requested.
Prepare a confederate block nurse who is administering light sedation (midazolam 1 mg already given) and can fetch the kit, draw drugs, and start CPR when directed.
Provide a syringe labeled ropivacaine 0.5% 30 mL attached to an echogenic block needle; the learner or a confederate regional attending is 20 mL into the injection when the scenario advances.
Brief learners: the patient is sedated but conversant, positioned semi-lateral for a right interscalene block before total shoulder arthroplasty.
Have the OR charge nurse reachable by phone for the cardiopulmonary bypass / perfusion notification if learners escalate.

patient.name: Margaret Ellison
medications: Acetaminophen as needed for shoulder pain
pmh: Severe right glenohumeral osteoarthritis scheduled for total shoulder arthroplasty
No prior problems with anesthesia; prior GA for cholecystectomy

airway.notes: Anticipated straightforward mask ventilation and laryngoscopy

patient.plan: Right interscalene brachial plexus block with ropivacaine 0.5% 30 mL (150 mg) under ultrasound guidance with light midazolam sedation, followed by general anesthesia for right total shoulder arthroplasty.

phases.label: Block Placement
phases.description: Ultrasound-guided interscalene block underway with ropivacaine 0.5%; patient lightly sedated and conversant.

phases.label: Prodromal Symptoms
phases.description: Perioral numbness, tinnitus, metallic taste, and agitation signal rising local anesthetic plasma levels; the injection must stop now.

phases.label: Seizure
phases.description: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure with airway compromise and desaturation; hypoxia and acidosis worsen cardiac toxicity.

phases.label: Cardiovascular Toxicity
phases.description: Conduction blockade and myocardial depression: widening QRS to ventricular tachycardia with profound hypotension, possibly arrest.

phases.label: Lipid Rescue and Modified ACLS
phases.description: Lipid emulsion bolus and infusion, benzodiazepine seizure control, CPR if pulseless, and reduced-dose epinephrine per ASRA.

phases.label: Stabilization and Monitoring
phases.description: Circulation recovers; the team plans prolonged monitoring, disposition, and case cancellation discussions.

events.label: Ropivacaine injection proceeding
events.description: The block needle is in place with good ultrasound imaging; 20 of 30 mL of ropivacaine 0.5% has been injected in 5 mL aliquots. Narrate normal spread; aspiration was reportedly negative, but a small vessel injection has occurred.

events.label: Prodromal neurologic symptoms
events.description: Patient states: 'My lips feel numb... there's a ringing in my ears... I feel funny, something is wrong.' She becomes restless and mildly agitated with a metallic taste. HR and BP drift up from the excitatory phase.

events.label: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
events.description: The patient stiffens and convulses. Respirations become erratic and ineffective; SpO2 falls into the upper 80s; HR 120. Capnography trace becomes chaotic. The seizure continues until treated with a benzodiazepine.

events.label: Cardiovascular toxicity: wide-complex VT and hypotension
events.description: Announce progressive QRS widening and PR prolongation on the monitor, then a wide-complex rhythm consistent with ventricular tachycardia. SBP collapses toward 60 with barely palpable pulses. This fires automatically if lipid has not been started; delay or advance it based on learner performance.

events.label: Cardiac arrest
events.description: Faculty-triggered if lipid emulsion is substantially delayed or hypoxia persists: pulseless with asystole on the monitor. Begin high-quality CPR; LAST arrests may require prolonged resuscitation because recovery is possible once the drug redistributes.

events.label: Response: benzodiazepine controls seizure
events.description: Trigger after midazolam 2 mg IV (or equivalent benzodiazepine): convulsions stop, respirations return with assisted bag-mask ventilation and 100% O2, and SpO2 recovers.

events.label: Response: 20% lipid emulsion bolus given
events.description: Trigger after learners push 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg (about 90 mL for 58 kg) over 2-3 minutes. Over the next 5 minutes the QRS narrows, VT converts to sinus tachycardia, and blood pressure climbs out of the critical range.

events.label: Response: lipid infusion with effective CPR (ROSC)
events.description: Trigger during an arrest once high-quality CPR, the lipid bolus (repeatable up to 3 doses), and an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min (about 15 mL/min) are running: return of spontaneous circulation with improving perfusion. Maximum total lipid about 12 mL/kg.

events.label: Response: small-dose epinephrine
events.description: Trigger when learners give epinephrine in reduced boluses of 1 mcg/kg or less (about 50 mcg here) per ASRA: modest BP support without precipitating refractory dysrhythmia. If learners give 1 mg boluses instead, warn of worsening ventricular dysrhythmias and impaired lipid efficacy.

events.label: Recurrent hypotension after initial recovery
events.description: Faculty-triggered if the team declares victory early, stops the lipid infusion, or plans immediate transfer without monitoring: BP sags again as local anesthetic redistributes, reinforcing the need for continued infusion and prolonged observation.

events.label: Response: sustained stabilization
events.description: Trigger once the lipid infusion continues at least 10 minutes past hemodynamic recovery and the team has a monitoring plan: vitals settle, the patient begins to arouse, and the team discusses ICU-level observation for 4-6 hours or more and cancellation of the arthroplasty.

expectedActions.label: Recognize prodromal LAST symptoms
expectedActions.description: Identifies perioral numbness, tinnitus, metallic taste, and agitation as signs of rising local anesthetic plasma levels rather than anxiety or oversedation.

expectedActions.label: Stop the injection immediately
expectedActions.description: Halts local anesthetic injection at the first symptom and withdraws the needle; states aloud that no further local anesthetic will be given.

expectedActions.label: Call for help and the LAST rescue kit
expectedActions.description: Declares a LAST emergency, summons additional staff, and requests the lipid rescue kit and the ASRA checklist.

expectedActions.label: Manage airway with 100% oxygen
expectedActions.description: Applies 100% oxygen with bag-mask support of ventilation to prevent hypoxia, hypercarbia, and acidosis, which potentiate cardiac toxicity; prepares intubation equipment.

expectedActions.label: Suppress seizure with a benzodiazepine
expectedActions.description: Gives midazolam 2 mg IV (or equivalent) as the first-line anticonvulsant; avoids large propofol doses given impending cardiovascular compromise.

expectedActions.label: Give 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus
expectedActions.description: Administers 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg (about 90 mL) IV over 2-3 minutes at the first sign of serious toxicity; may repeat up to twice for persistent instability.

expectedActions.label: Start lipid infusion
expectedActions.description: Follows the bolus with an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min (about 15 mL/min), doubling for refractory hypotension, continuing at least 10 minutes past hemodynamic recovery, maximum about 12 mL/kg total.

expectedActions.label: Start CPR immediately if pulseless
expectedActions.description: Begins high-quality chest compressions without delay when pulses are lost, verbalizing that circulation is required for lipid to reach the heart.

expectedActions.label: Use reduced epinephrine doses
expectedActions.description: Limits epinephrine boluses to 1 mcg/kg or less (about 50 mcg) per ASRA rather than standard 1 mg ACLS doses.

expectedActions.label: Avoid vasopressin, CCBs, beta-blockers, and lidocaine
expectedActions.description: Explicitly avoids vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and lidocaine (and other local anesthetics) during the resuscitation; uses amiodarone if an antiarrhythmic is needed.

expectedActions.label: Notify cardiopulmonary bypass team early
expectedActions.description: Alerts the nearest facility or team capable of cardiopulmonary bypass / ECMO early in a refractory arrest, anticipating prolonged resuscitation.

expectedActions.label: Plan monitoring for at least 4-6 hours
expectedActions.description: Arranges monitored (ICU-level) observation for at least 4-6 hours after a cardiovascular event (2 hours after a limited CNS event) because instability can recur as lipid clears.

expectedActions.label: Communicate with surgeon and family; defer surgery
expectedActions.description: Informs the surgical team and family, cancels or postpones the arthroplasty, documents the event and total lipid dose, and reports to a LAST registry if available.

expectedProgression: Minutes 0-2: block in progress; 20 of 30 mL ropivacaine 0.5% injected with reassuring ultrasound images and reportedly negative aspiration.

Minutes 2-5: prodrome — perioral numbness, tinnitus, metallic taste, agitation with mild hypertension and tachycardia; learners should stop the injection, call for help, and bring the lipid kit to the bedside.
Minutes 5-8: generalized tonic-clonic seizure with erratic ineffective respirations and desaturation into the upper 80s; benzodiazepine and supported ventilation with 100% oxygen are required.
Minutes 8-12: cardiovascular toxicity — progressive QRS widening to ventricular tachycardia with SBP near 60; lipid emulsion bolus is due now if not already given.

If lipid is delayed or hypoxia persists, the patient arrests (asystole), requiring prolonged CPR, repeated lipid boluses, an infusion, and reduced-dose epinephrine, with early bypass/ECMO notification.
With correct therapy, the rhythm narrows and converts and blood pressure recovers over about 5 minutes after the bolus; premature discontinuation of the infusion produces recurrent hypotension.
Stabilization: patient arouses, the case is deferred, and the team arranges at least 4-6 hours of monitored observation and full documentation.
correctManagement: Inject local anesthetic in small (3-5 mL) aliquots with aspiration and ongoing verbal contact; treat any new neurologic complaint during injection as LAST until proven otherwise.
Stop the injection immediately at the first prodromal sign and call for help and the LAST rescue kit with the ASRA checklist.
Maintain oxygenation and ventilation with 100% oxygen and bag-mask support; intubate if the airway cannot otherwise be protected, because hypoxia and acidosis dramatically worsen cardiac toxicity.
Terminate seizures with benzodiazepines first-line (midazolam 1-2 mg IV increments); use only small doses of propofol, if any, once cardiovascular signs are present.
Give 20% lipid emulsion early: 1.5 mL/kg (about 90 mL for 58 kg) IV over 2-3 minutes at the first sign of serious toxicity, not only after arrest.

Follow with a lipid infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min (about 15 mL/min), repeat the bolus up to two more times and double the infusion for refractory collapse, continue at least 10 minutes past recovery, and respect the approximately 12 mL/kg total ceiling.
If pulseless, begin high-quality CPR immediately and expect a prolonged resuscitation; circulation is what carries lipid to the myocardium.
Modify ACLS per ASRA: epinephrine boluses of 1 mcg/kg or less, no vasopressin, no calcium channel blockers or beta-blockers, and no lidocaine; prefer amiodarone for ventricular dysrhythmias.
Notify the nearest cardiopulmonary bypass / ECMO capable team early in a refractory arrest.
After recovery, continue monitored observation for at least 4-6 hours following cardiovascular involvement, since toxicity can recur as lipid partitioning shifts.
Defer the planned surgery, communicate transparently with the surgeon, patient, and family, and document drug, dose, timing, and total lipid administered.

Reduce future risk: weight- and comorbidity-adjusted dosing in small elderly patients, ultrasound guidance, incremental injection, and an immediately available lipid kit wherever blocks are performed.
commonErrors: Dismissing perioral numbness, tinnitus, or agitation as anxiety or oversedation and continuing the injection.
Delaying lipid emulsion until cardiac arrest instead of giving it at the first sign of serious toxicity.
Treating the seizure with a large propofol bolus in a patient on the verge of cardiovascular collapse.
Using standard 1 mg epinephrine doses, vasopressin, or lidocaine during the resuscitation, contrary to ASRA guidance.
Forgetting the lipid infusion after the bolus, or stopping it immediately upon recovery, allowing recurrent instability.
Neglecting ventilation and oxygenation, letting hypoxia and acidosis potentiate cardiac toxicity.
Abandoning resuscitation early; LAST arrests can recover after prolonged CPR, and bypass/ECMO should be considered rather than termination.
Discharging or transferring the patient to an unmonitored setting shortly after apparent recovery.

points: Mechanism of LAST: sodium-channel blockade in the CNS (excitation then depression) and myocardium (conduction block and contractile failure); ropivacaine is less cardiotoxic than bupivacaine but 150 mg intravascularly in a 58 kg elderly patient is ample for toxicity.
Risk factors present in this case: advanced age, low body weight, relatively high milligram dose, and a vessel-rich interscalene site; how dose adjustment and incremental injection reduce risk.

The rationale for immediate lipid therapy at first serious signs, the dosing sequence (1.5 mL/kg bolus, 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion, redosing, 12 mL/kg ceiling), and the leading 'lipid sink'/metabolic hypotheses.
Why ASRA modifies ACLS in LAST: small epinephrine doses, avoidance of vasopressin, CCBs, beta-blockers, and lidocaine, and early bypass/ECMO notification for refractory arrest.
The primacy of oxygenation and seizure control: hypoxia and acidosis multiply cardiac toxicity, so airway management is itself cardiotoxicity treatment.
Systems safety: lipid kits and cognitive aids physically present in every block area, checklist use under stress, and closed-loop communication during the resuscitation.
Post-event duties: 4-6 hours minimum monitored observation, documentation of doses and timing, disclosure to the patient and family, and case deferral decisions.
questions: What did you make of her first complaints, and what made you commit (or hesitate to commit) to the diagnosis of LAST?
How did you prioritize airway, seizure control, and lipid emulsion in the first two minutes, and would you sequence anything differently?
Talk through your lipid dosing: how you calculated it, when you would redose, and when you would stop the infusion.
How does an arrest from LAST change your ACLS drug choices and your expectations about resuscitation duration?
What preventive steps in block technique or dosing might have changed the course for this 58 kg patient?
Where is the lipid kit in your own block area, and who would you assign to mix and run it in a real event?
What does this patient need from the team over the next 6 hours, and how would you handle the conversation with her family and the surgeon?
